locked_facts: Surface tension T = force per unit length along the surface (N/m)
Surface energy = T × area (surface tension = surface energy per unit area)
Excess pressure inside a soap bubble: ΔP = 4T/R (two surfaces)
Excess pressure inside a liquid drop: ΔP = 2T/R (one surface)
Capillary rise: h = 2T cosθ/(ρgr) where r is tube radius, θ is contact angle
Contact angle θ < 90°: liquid wets the surface (concave meniscus) — water-glass
Contact angle θ > 90°: liquid doesn't wet (convex meniscus) — mercury-glass
truth_statements: Surface tension arises because molecules at the surface have fewer neighbours and hence higher energy than molecules in the bulk
A soap bubble has two liquid surfaces so the excess pressure inside is 4T/R, twice that of a liquid drop
Capillary rise is inversely proportional to tube radius — narrower tubes show greater rise
The contact angle determines whether the meniscus is concave (wetting) or convex (non-wetting)
Surface tension decreases with increasing temperature as molecular cohesion weakens

aha_moment.what_to_show: Three capillary tubes of different radii in water: narrow tube shows high rise, medium tube moderate rise, wide tube barely any rise. h = 2Tcosθ/(ρgr) — the 1/r dependence is visually obvious. Trees use this to transport water!
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Three tubes of decreasing radius with increasing water heights, h ∝ 1/r clearly demonstrated
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students think water rises the same height in all tubes — the dramatic difference with radius makes the 1/r relationship memorable

simulation_states.label: Surface tension concept
simulation_states.description: Molecules at the surface vs bulk. Surface molecules have net inward pull → surface behaves like a stretched membrane. Needle floating on water due to surface tension.
simulation_states.emphasis: Surface molecules have unbalanced forces → the surface acts like an elastic sheet

simulation_states.label: Excess pressure
simulation_states.description: A liquid drop and a soap bubble shown in cross-section. Drop: ΔP = 2T/R (one surface). Bubble: ΔP = 4T/R (two surfaces). Smaller radius → higher excess pressure.
simulation_states.emphasis: Bubble has TWO surfaces → twice the excess pressure of a drop

simulation_states.label: Capillary rise
simulation_states.description: Water in glass tubes of different radii. Narrower tube → higher rise. h = 2Tcosθ/(ρgr). Mercury in glass: depression (θ > 90°, convex meniscus).
simulation_states.emphasis: Rise ∝ 1/r — halve the radius, double the rise. Mercury dips instead of rising.

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts tube radius and selects liquid (water or mercury). Capillary rise/depression shown. Contact angle and meniscus shape change. Excess pressure in drops/bubbles adjustable.
simulation_states.emphasis: Change radius — see rise change as 1/r. Switch to mercury — the meniscus flips!

common_misconceptions.belief: A soap bubble has the same excess pressure as a liquid drop of the same radius
common_misconceptions.correction: A soap bubble has TWO liquid-air surfaces (inner and outer), so ΔP = 4T/R. A liquid drop has only ONE surface, so ΔP = 2T/R. The bubble has twice the excess pressure.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show cross-section of bubble with two surfaces labelled vs drop with one surface

common_misconceptions.belief: Capillary rise is caused by atmospheric pressure pushing water up
common_misconceptions.correction: Capillary rise is caused by adhesive forces between water and glass creating a concave meniscus. Surface tension along the meniscus pulls the water up. Atmospheric pressure plays a secondary role in preventing vacuum formation.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show adhesive forces at contact → meniscus formation → surface tension pulling upward
